examples.title: Generic Swap Function
examples.description: A function template to swap two values of any type
examples.code:
// Function Template for Swap
TEMPLATE<typename T>
VOID swap(T& a, T& b)
BEGIN
 T temp = a
 a = b
 b = temp
END

// Usage
INT x = 5, y = 10
swap(x, y) // T deduced as int

DOUBLE p = 3.14, q = 2.71
swap(p, q) // T deduced as double

STRING s1 = "Hello", s2 = "World"
swap(s1, s2) // T deduced as string
examples.explanation: The template parameter T is replaced with the actual type at compile time. Both reference parameters ensure actual values are swapped, not copies.

examples.title: Generic Array Maximum Finder
examples.description: Function template to find maximum element in an array
examples.code:
// Function Template for Finding Maximum
TEMPLATE<typename T, std::size_t N>
T findMax(T arr[N])
BEGIN
 T maxVal = arr[0]
 FOR i FROM 1 TO N-1
 BEGIN
 IF arr[i] > maxVal THEN
 maxVal = arr[i]
 END
 END
 RETURN maxVal
END

// Usage with Different Types
INT numbers[5] = {3, 7, 2, 9, 1}
INT maxInt = findMax(numbers) // Returns 9

DOUBLE decimals[4] = {1.5, 3.2, 0.8, 2.9}
DOUBLE maxDouble = findMax(decimals) // Returns 3.2
examples.explanation: Uses both type parameter T and non-type parameter N for array size. Works with any type that supports comparison operator >.

examples.title: Template Specialization Example
examples.description: Primary template and specialized version for character pointers
examples.code:
// Primary Template
TEMPLATE<typename T>
T absoluteValue(T value)
BEGIN
 IF value < 0 THEN
 RETURN -value
 END
 RETURN value
END

// Full Specialization for char*
TEMPLATE<>
CHAR* absoluteValue<CHAR*>(CHAR* value)
BEGIN
 RETURN value // For strings, just return as-is
END

// Usage
INT i = -5
INT absInt = absoluteValue(i) // Calls primary, returns 5

DOUBLE d = -3.14
DOUBLE absDouble = absoluteValue(d) // Calls primary, returns 3.14

CHAR* str = "hello"
CHAR* absStr = absoluteValue(str) // Calls specialized version
examples.explanation: Specialization allows providing custom implementation for specific types while using the general template for all other types.

examples.title: Variadic Template for Sum
examples.description: Variable number of arguments sum using recursive template
examples.code:
// Base case: empty parameter pack
TEMPLATE<typename T>
T sum(T value)
BEGIN
 RETURN value
END

// Recursive case: process first arg, recurse on rest
TEMPLATE<typename T, typename... Args>
T sum(T first, Args... args)
BEGIN
 RETURN first + sum(args...)
END

// Usage
INT result1 = sum(1, 2, 3, 4, 5) // Returns 15
DOUBLE result2 = sum(1.5, 2.5, 3.0) // Returns 7.0
MIXED result3 = sum(1, 2.5, 3) // Returns 6.5
examples.explanation: Variadic templates use parameter packs (...) to handle any number of arguments. Recursion processes one argument at a time until base case handles the last one.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of arguments

examples.title: Default Template Arguments
examples.description: Function template with default type parameter
examples.code:
// Template with default type
TEMPLATE<typename T = INT>
T multiply(T a, T b = T(1))
BEGIN
 RETURN a * b
END

// Usage
INT m1 = multiply(5, 3) // Both args specified, returns 15
INT m2 = multiply(5) // Uses default b=1, returns 5
DOUBLE m3 = multiply(2.5, 3.0) // Uses double, returns 7.5
DOUBLE m4 = multiply<double>(5, 2) // Explicit double, returns 10
examples.explanation: Default template arguments provide fallback types when not specified. Function default arguments work with template arguments for flexible calling.
